title: Typography & Readability
summary: Typography accounts for 95% of web design. Line length, leading, weight contrast, and hierarchy determine whether text gets read or skipped.
rules: Line length: 60-80 characters (45-75ch) for body text
Line height: 1.5-1.7x for body, 1.1-1.3x for headings
dos: Use system fonts or a well-known variable font for performance
Increase font size on mobile (16px minimum)
Use real typographic hierarchy: H1 > H2 > body is visually distinct
donts: Don't use more than 2 font families
Don't use all-caps for body text
Don't set line height below 1.4 for body copy

title: Color & Contrast (WCAG)
summary: Color contrast isn't just accessibility — it's the primary driver of legibility. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text.
rules: Normal text: minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio (WCAG AA)
Never use color as the only signal — pair with shape, text, or pattern
Test all states: default, hover, focus, disabled, error
dos: Use a contrast checker on every text/background combination
Pair color with icons for status (error = red + X icon)
donts: Don't use light gray text on white background
Don't use only color to indicate error state
Don't use red/green as the only distinction

title: Color Psychology & Palette Strategy
summary: Colors carry universal meaning that sets emotional tone before content is read. Blue = trust, Green = growth, Red = urgency, Purple = premium.
rules: Choose your primary color based on the emotional contract with your user
Limit palette to: 1 primary, 1 accent, neutrals, semantic colors
Dark mode requires separate contrast validation
Semantic colors must be culturally consistent
dos: Use your primary color for the most important interactive element only
Test your palette with a color blindness simulator
Define your palette as design tokens
donts: Don't use more than 5 distinct colors in a single view
Don't use pure black (#000) — use near-black (#111)
Don't use color trends that clash with your brand personality
